questions.prompt: Why can't Dart code in a Flutter app directly call native platform code the way, say, Kotlin can call Java on Android?

options.text: Flutter paints its own UI via Skia/Impeller and runs Dart in its own runtime, separate from the native platform's runtime, with no shared memory — any interaction must cross this boundary via explicit message-passing, not a direct call.
Dart is a sandboxed, JavaScript-like scripting language that cannot execute compiled native operations at all.
This limitation only applies to iOS; on Android, Dart and native code can call each other directly without any bridge.
Platform channels are an artificial restriction that Google could remove in a future Flutter release with no architectural changes required.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's precise, correct explanation, explicitly contrasted against a common misconception: the boundary exists because of separate runtimes with no shared memory, a genuine architectural fact, not an arbitrary restriction. (b) is explicitly named and rejected in the chapter's Interview lens as the WRONG mental model — Dart compiles to native machine code, it is not a sandboxed scripting language. (c) is false; the separate-runtime boundary applies identically on both iOS and Android — platform channels exist for both. (d) mischaracterizes a fundamental architectural constraint (separate runtimes, no shared memory) as an arbitrary, easily-removable restriction.

questions.prompt: Why must data passed through a MethodChannel be restricted to platform-standard types (numbers, strings, booleans, lists, maps) rather than arbitrary Dart objects?

options.text: Dart and native code share no memory, so a Dart object reference is meaningless on the native side — only data that both sides know how to encode and decode identically (i.e. serializable, standard types) can actually cross the boundary.
This is an arbitrary API design choice by the Flutter team that could easily be changed to support arbitrary objects.
Arbitrary Dart objects are too large in memory to be transmitted over any channel.
This restriction only applies to EventChannel, not MethodChannel.

questions.explanation: This is a direct, necessary consequence of the no-shared-memory fact established earlier in the chapter — object REFERENCES are meaningless across a boundary with no shared address space, so only self-contained, encodable data can cross. (b) mischaracterizes a fundamental architectural constraint as an arbitrary, changeable API decision. (c) is false and not the actual reason; the constraint is about reference-passing being meaningless across runtimes, not about data size. (d) is false — the chapter explicitly states EventChannel uses 'the same underlying message-passing mechanism, same serialization constraint' as MethodChannel.

questions.prompt: What is the actual relationship between MethodChannel and EventChannel?

options.text: EventChannel is really MethodChannel's handshake (start/stop listening) plus a way for native code to push messages back repeatedly, rather than only once in response to a Dart-initiated call — same underlying mechanism, different communication shape (streaming vs. request/response).
They are entirely unrelated APIs implemented via completely different underlying technology.
EventChannel is a deprecated predecessor to MethodChannel and should not be used in new code.
MethodChannel can only be used on Android, while EventChannel can only be used on iOS.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's explicit characterization — EventChannel isn't a fundamentally different bridge, it's the same message-passing mechanism adapted for continuous/streaming data instead of one-shot request/response. (b) directly contradicts the chapter's explicit statement about shared mechanism. (c) is false; EventChannel is the current, actively-used mechanism for streaming platform data (sensors, location), not a deprecated one. (d) is false and a fabricated platform restriction; both channel types are available on both Android and iOS.

questions.prompt: A screen freezes and drops frames while parsing a large JSON payload, even though no platform channel call is involved anywhere in this code. What's the cause, and the fix?

options.text: Dart is single-threaded per isolate, so a long-running synchronous computation blocks the UI isolate's event loop directly — unrelated to platform channels; compute() runs the function on a separate isolate, keeping the UI isolate responsive.
This is caused by a platform channel timeout, since all Dart computation implicitly goes through MethodChannel.
JSON parsing in Dart always requires native code, so this is actually a platform channel performance issue.
The fix works by running expensiveParse on a background native thread using MethodChannel internally.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's explicit distinction: this freeze has NOTHING to do with platform channels — it's Dart's own single-threaded-per-isolate concurrency model blocking the UI event loop with synchronous CPU work, a separate concept the chapter deliberately introduces after platform channels precisely to avoid conflating the two. (b) and (c) both incorrectly attribute an isolate-level concurrency issue to the platform channel mechanism, which the chapter explicitly separates as a different concept entirely. (d) mischaracterizes compute()'s actual mechanism — it uses Dart isolates (a Dart-level concurrency primitive), not MethodChannel or native threads.

questions.prompt: Why must the function passed to compute() be a top-level or static function, rather than a closure that captures local variables from its surrounding scope?

options.text: Isolates communicate only via serializable messages, never shared references — a closure capturing local state (which may include non-serializable objects or references) can't be transmitted across the isolate boundary the same way a MethodChannel call can't pass a Dart object reference to native code.
This is an arbitrary Dart language restriction with no relationship to how isolates actually work.
Closures are slower than top-level functions, so this restriction exists purely for performance reasons.
Static functions are required because compute() is itself implemented using platform channels internally.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's explicit parallel between isolate boundaries and platform channel boundaries — both share the 'only serializable messages, never shared references' constraint, which is exactly why a closure's captured environment (which can't be serialized in general) can't cross into the new isolate. (b) understates a real, mechanistic reason tied directly to isolate memory isolation, not an arbitrary language quirk. (c) misattributes the restriction to performance when it's actually about serialization feasibility — a closure isn't merely 'slower,' its captured state often literally cannot be encoded to cross the isolate boundary at all. (d) is false; compute() uses Dart's isolate mechanism, not platform channels, which the chapter treats as two distinct concepts.

questions.prompt: A team wraps every async function in their app — including simple network API calls — with compute(), reasoning 'isolates make async code faster.' Is this a sound practice per the chapter?

options.text: No — compute()/isolates have real overhead (isolate spin-up, serializing input/output) that's only worth paying for genuinely CPU-bound work; ordinary I/O-bound async work like network calls is already non-blocking via Dart's event loop and gains nothing from an isolate.
Yes — wrapping all async operations in compute() is always beneficial and has no downsides.
This is required by Flutter; any async function not wrapped in compute() will be rejected by the framework.
This only matters for apps targeting iOS; on Android, compute() has no overhead at all.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's explicit closing trade-off: isolates solve a specific problem (CPU-bound work blocking the event loop) and have real costs of their own — applying them to I/O-bound work that was never blocking the UI in the first place adds overhead for zero benefit. (b) is exactly the misconception the chapter's trade-off paragraph corrects. (c) is fabricated; ordinary async functions using await work perfectly well without compute() and are not rejected by the framework. (d) fabricates a platform-specific exemption from isolate overhead that doesn't exist; the cost applies on both platforms.
